hypothesis: Keltner channel breakouts signal a shift in volatility and price direction; PSAR confirms the trend direction at the moment of the breakout; ATR-based risk limits provide tight, volatility‑adjusted stops and realistic profit targets, reducing false signals in choppy markets.

Using Keltner band breakouts with PSAR trend confirmation filters out false breakouts that often occur in sideways markets; ATR-based stop and take‑profit adapt risk to current volatility, reducing drawdowns while preserving reward potential.

A breakout from the Keltner channel combined with PSAR trend confirmation reduces whipsaws; ATR-based risk limits ensure positions are exited before significant retracements, improving Sharpe and reducing drawdown.

A breakout beyond the Keltner upper or lower band is more likely to sustain a move when the price is already above the band’s middle line, indicating a prevailing trend; confirming the PSAR direction further reduces false signals, while ATR-based SL/TP manages volatility exposure.
Keltner band breakouts are most reliable when a strong trend exists; filtering with ADX > 25 removes weak‑trend false signals, while ATR‑based stops adapt to volatility and prevent large drawdowns.

A breakout above or below the Keltner band signals a move into a new volatility regime; confirming the direction with the PSAR reduces false breakouts that occur in sideways markets, while an ATR-based stop and take‑profit protects against over‑exposure to sudden volatility spikes.
